name: Behavioral Change & Habit Frameworks
description: Habit Loop, Fogg Behavior Model, and Four Tendencies for designing and sustaining behavioral change.
when_to_use: User wants to build or break a habit
Task involves creating sustainable routines or systems
Agent needs to design a behavior change intervention
User keeps starting but not sustaining new practices
Need to understand why someone resists or follows through on commitments

habit_loop.name: The Habit Loop (Charles Duhigg)
habit_loop.description: Every habit follows: Cue → Routine → Reward. Understanding this loop is the foundation for change.
habit_loop.best_for: Breaking bad habits, installing new habits, understanding automatic behaviors

cue.description: The trigger that initiates the behavior
questions: What triggers this behavior? When does it happen?
What time of day? Where? Who is around?
What emotion or state precedes it?

routine.description: The behavior itself — the habit you want to change or install
questions: What does it look like step by step?
Can this routine be made simpler or replaced with a better one?

reward.description: The benefit that reinforces the behavior
questions: What need does this behavior satisfy?
What feeling do you get during or after?
Could a different routine provide the same reward?
Is the reward immediate or delayed? (Immediate rewards drive habit formation)

change_protocol: Identify the routine you want to change
Experiment with different rewards to find what craving drives the habit
Isolate the cue (time, place, emotion, people, preceding action)
Create a plan: When [CUE], I will [NEW ROUTINE] because it provides [REWARD]

fogg.name: Fogg Behavior Model (BJ Fogg)
fogg.description: Behavior = Motivation + Ability + Prompt. All three must converge at the same moment.
fogg.best_for: Designing new behaviors, understanding why behaviors fail, making change easy

motivation.description: The desire to do the behavior
boosters: Connect to something they already care about
Make the outcome vivid and personal
Reduce the gap between effort and reward

ability.description: How easy the behavior is to do
simplicity_factors: Time — how long does it take?
Physical effort — how hard is it?
Mental effort — how much thinking is required?
Social deviance — does it go against norms?
Routine disruption — does it break existing patterns?
make_easier: Shrink the behavior to its tiniest version

prompt.description: The signal to do the behavior NOW

types.action: Anchor to an existing habit (after I [existing], I will [new])

prompt.design_principle: The best prompt is an existing behavior you already do reliably

fogg.tiny_habits_recipe: After I [ANCHOR HABIT], I will [TINY NEW BEHAVIOR]. Then I celebrate.
failure_diagnosis: If behavior isn't happening: which component is missing?
Low motivation → make it more meaningful or connected to values
Low ability → make it easier, smaller, remove barriers
No prompt → attach to an existing reliable cue

four_tendencies.name: The Four Tendencies (Gretchen Rubin)
four_tendencies.description: How people respond to inner and outer expectations determines which strategies work for them.
four_tendencies.best_for: Personalizing habit and accountability strategies based on how someone is motivated

upholder.description: Meets both outer and inner expectations. Self-motivated, follows through.
upholder.strategy: Give clear expectations and let them execute. They thrive with structure.
upholder.pitfall: Can be rigid. May need permission to adapt or break rules when appropriate.

questioner.description: Meets inner expectations, resists outer. Needs to understand WHY.
questioner.strategy: Always explain the reasoning. Provide data and evidence. Let them customize.
questioner.pitfall: Analysis paralysis. May need a deadline to stop researching and start acting.

obliger.description: Meets outer expectations, struggles with inner. Needs external accountability.
obliger.strategy: Build in accountability partners, deadlines, and check-ins. Make commitments to others.
obliger.pitfall: Can burn out serving others. Needs permission to prioritize self.

rebel.description: Resists both inner and outer expectations. Driven by identity and freedom.
rebel.strategy: Frame as identity ('I'm the kind of person who...'), not obligation. Offer choices, never ultimatums.
rebel.pitfall: May resist even their own goals. Needs autonomy and variety.

detection_questions: Do you follow through on commitments to yourself as easily as commitments to others?
Do you need to understand WHY before you'll do something?
Do you find it easier to keep promises to others than to yourself?
Do you resist expectations even when they come from yourself?
